sources.title: Prevalence and characteristics of peanut allergy in US adults
sources.journal: Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology

download.filename: Prevalence and characteristics of peanut allergy in US adults.pdf

sources.relevance: Establishes the magnitude of the peanut allergy burden — ~1.8% convincing prevalence in US adults, ~800,000 with adult-onset. Provides context for the "how big is this problem" question but does not directly address vaccine causation.
sources.html_filename: Prevalence and characteristics of peanut allergy in US adults.html

sources.title: The Allergy Epidemics: 1870-2010
sources.journal: Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology
sources.relevance: Authoritative review tracing three sequential allergy epidemics. Attributes them primarily to lifestyle changes (hygiene, indoor living, skin exposure). Notes vaccine formulation changes (whole-cell → acellular pertussis in 1992) as potentially relevant to food allergy timing but emphasizes no proper controlled trial exists. Does NOT endorse vaccines as a primary cause.

sources.title: Evidence That Food Proteins in Vaccines Cause the Development of Food Allergies and Its Implications for Vaccine Policy
sources.found_via: User-provided Scribd link; full text retrieved from Longdom open access
sources.relevance: Central to the hypothesis under investigation. Claims that trace food proteins (ovalbumin, casein, gelatin, soy) in vaccines, combined with aluminum adjuvants, cause IgE-mediated food allergy. Cites Wells 1908 (50ng ovalbumin sensitization in guinea pigs) and 2011 IOM report. Published in a low-impact open-access journal (Longdom/OMICS group — known predatory publisher). The claims are mechanistically plausible but the source quality is poor.

sources.title: Aluminum Adjuvant-Containing Vaccines in the Context of the Hygiene Hypothesis: A Risk Factor for Eosinophilia and Allergy in a Genetically Susceptible Subpopulation?
sources.journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
sources.relevance: Proposes that aluminum adjuvants trigger Th2-skewed immune responses without regulatory T cell activation, potentially increasing allergy risk in genetically susceptible children. Relies primarily on mouse models. Explicitly acknowledges this is a hypothesis requiring validation, not proven causation. 119 references.

sources.title: Anaphylaxis to additives in vaccines
sources.relevance: Comprehensive review of allergenic vaccine excipients (gelatin, ovalbumin, casein, PEG, polysorbate 80). Documents anaphylaxis rates of 1-10 per million vaccinations. Key data: ovalbumin limit ≤1 µg/dose, bovine casein detected in nanogram amounts in DTaP. Distinguishes between rare anaphylactic reactions (well-documented) and the broader question of whether vaccines drive population-level allergy sensitization (not addressed).

sources.title: Timing of routine infant vaccinations and risk of food allergy and eczema at one year of age
sources.relevance: Population-based cohort of 4,433 children. Delayed DTaP was NOT associated with food allergy (p=0.49) but WAS associated with reduced eczema (p=0.04) and eczema medication use (p=0.01).

sources.title: Timeliness of DTaP-IPV-Hib Vaccination and Development of Atopic Dermatitis Between 4 Months and 1 Year of Age—Register-Based Cohort Study
sources.relevance: Massive cohort: 883,160 Danish children (1997-2012). Delayed DTaP associated with reduced atopic dermatitis: aRR 0.94 (0.91-0.97) for delayed first dose; aRR 0.88 (0.82-0.93) for delayed first and second doses. Dose-response relationship strengthens causal inference.

sources.title: Whole-Cell Pertussis Vaccination and Decreased Risk of IgE-Mediated Food Allergy: A Nested Case-Control Study
sources.relevance: Australian children born 1997-1999 during wP→aP transition. Infants receiving whole-cell pertussis (wP) had OR 0.77 (95% CI 0.62-0.95) for IgE-mediated food allergy vs. acellular pertussis (aP). Suggests vaccine *formulation* matters more than vaccination itself.

sources.title: Infant Whole-Cell Versus Acellular Pertussis Vaccination in 1997 to 1999 and Risk of Childhood Hospitalization for Food-Induced Anaphylaxis
sources.relevance: Follow-up to #8. 218,093 NSW children. wP recipients had food anaphylaxis rate of 3.5 vs 5.1 per 100,000 child-years (aP); adjusted HR 0.47 (95% CI 0.26-0.83). This is a striking 53% reduction in food anaphylaxis hospitalization with whole-cell vaccine.

sources.title: Vaccination and Allergic Disease: A Birth Cohort Study
sources.relevance: 29,238 children. Initial vaccine-allergy association vanished after adjusting for GP visit frequency. 83% of unvaccinated children were in the lowest quartile of doctor visits. Concluded the association was ascertainment bias: unvaccinated children simply got diagnosed less.

sources.title: Vaccination Status and Health in Children and Adolescents (KiGGS)
sources.relevance: 13,453 German children (1-17 years). Found NO statistically significant differences in atopic disorder prevalence between vaccinated and unvaccinated. Some raw numbers showed higher rates in vaccinated children for certain age groups but not after adjustment.

sources.title: Pilot comparative study on the health of vaccinated and unvaccinated 6- to 12-year-old U.S. children
sources.relevance: 666 homeschooled children via parental survey. Reported higher allergy/asthma rates in vaccinated. Retracted from Frontiers in Public Health for methodological flaws. Republished in low-impact journal. Major issues: parental recall bias, no medical record verification, reviewed by a chiropractor, self-selected homeschool sample. Widely criticized by mainstream science.

sources.title: Effect of endotoxin and alum adjuvant vaccine on peanut allergy
sources.relevance: Mouse model. Alum/CpG adjuvant combination reduced peanut-induced allergic responses compared to alum alone. Suggests adjuvant formulation modifications could reduce allergy risk. Authors note: "modification of childhood vaccine adjuvant formulations may provide a method to reduce the development/severity of allergic disease."

sources.title: Historical Comparisons of Morbidity and Mortality for Vaccine-Preventable Diseases in the United States
sources.relevance: Essential context for the risk-benefit analysis. Documents >92% decline in cases and ≥99% decline in deaths for diphtheria, mumps, pertussis, tetanus after vaccination. ~103 million cases prevented since 1924.
